narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and notably low LSAT score of 20, while planning to attend full-time after ranking in the 2nd decile for your first-year school grades and the 1st decile for your third-year performance. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 64%, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of students struggling post-graduation. This decision hinged most heavily on your third-year school decile ranking, which stood out as unusually low compared to the typical student's 5th or 6th decile placement and strongly signaled a higher risk of bar exam challenges. Your LSAT score, well below the average of around 37, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA, though below the dataset average of 3.21, helped counterbalance some of that risk by suggesting solid potential. Similarly, your upper-middle family income provided a modest boost, likely reflecting access to resources that aid success, while your lower first-year decile and full-time study plan offered smaller positive influences—full-time being the norm for 93% of students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker academic deciles and LSAT performance that deviated sharply from the stronger averages, outweighing your other strengths. We understand this rejection feels disheartening, especially given your dedication, and it's not a judgment on your character or potential as a white male from a supportive background. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 37 average, bolstering your school grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and highlighting any extenuating circumstances in a reapplication. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas—reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice, and keep pursuing your legal ambitions with persistence. You've got this potential; this is just one data point on your journey.
